turns.content: I'm trying to make a POST request using reqwest but my code just hangs forever. I set up the client and added headers, yet nothing happens.

The issue is likely that you haven't actually awaited the async function or configured the timeout properly. When you call Client::new() in reqwest, it creates an async handle that needs to be used with .get(), .post(), etc., but those methods return a future that must be awaited before the request completes. Without awaiting, your program waits indefinitely because the HTTP handshake never finishes.
I know I need to await it, but what exactly should I do with the response? It seems like I'm missing something about how reqwest handles the connection pool.

The connection pool in reqwest is managed automatically by Client::new() and you don't need to configure it manually unless you have very specific performance needs. When you await a request like .post(), the library handles keeping connections alive between requests, which improves efficiency. However, if your code hangs, check that you're not forgetting the timeout configuration using Config::timeout() or letting the default infinite wait apply when the server doesn't respond.

Ah, I see now. So even though I'm setting headers correctly with Client::new(), the real problem was that I wasn't awaiting and also didn't set a timeout. How do I properly structure this so it doesn't hang?

You should create your client once, then use the async methods with proper timeouts. For example, you would call let response = reqwest::Client::new().post(url).header("Content-Type", "application/json").timeout(Duration::from_secs(30)).send(body).await?; This ensures that if the server takes too long to respond, your program will fail gracefully instead of hanging forever.
That makes sense. But what happens to the headers I set? Do they persist across multiple requests or do I need to redefine them every time?

Headers are set per-request when you call methods like .post() or .put(). You don't attach them once globally unless you use a builder pattern where you chain multiple configuration steps. Each request is independent, so if you want to send the same headers repeatedly, just include them in every request method call. The Client::new() instance itself manages the underlying TCP connections and pool, but individual HTTP requests are separate operations.
Okay, so I understand now. I was confusing the client initialization with per-request configuration. One more thing though - what if I want to send a PUT request instead?

PUT works exactly like POST in reqwest; you just change the method from .post() to .put(). The underlying Client::new() handles both methods identically regarding connection pooling and timeout. You can chain headers, set timeouts, and send data the same way: let response = client.put(url).header("X-Custom", "value").timeout(Duration::from_secs(60)).send(payload).await?;

I'm getting this error: 'the trait bound for reqwest::block::Client as std::convert::Into<reqwest::blocking::Client> is not satisfied'. I tried to use the blocking client but it seems incompatible. What went wrong with my setup?

Hey! That error happens because you're mixing async and blocking APIs incorrectly. The trait bound fails since reqwest::blocking::Client doesn't implement Into for the async version directly in your code context. You need to explicitly convert it or just use the async client from the start if you want to avoid that conversion step entirely.
Oh, I see. But how do I actually set up the blocking client properly? Do I just import reqwest::blocking and call Client::new()? Can I still use it for my proof-of-concept?

Yes, you can absolutely use the blocking client for your PoC. Just add `use reqwest::blocking;` at the top and then call `Client::new()` to get a standard blocking HTTP client instance. This gives you all the basic methods like GET/POST without needing async syntax.

Great, so if I want to do a POST request with custom headers on that blocking client, what's the exact way to chain those calls? I don't want to miss anything important like timeout or connection pooling settings.

You can call `.post(url).header(key, value)` to set headers, then `.timeout(duration)` for timeouts. For connection pooling, you usually configure it via the builder pattern before creating the client instance with `Client::builder().connect_timeout(...)`. Just make sure all these methods are chained correctly on the blocking Client object.
Wait a second! I thought connection pooling was only for async clients. Is it really possible to configure it with the blocking client too? That feels like a big win if true!

You're absolutely right to question that—it's a common misconception! Yes, you can configure connection pooling on the blocking client using `Client::builder().connect_timeout()` and other settings. It works exactly the same way as in async mode because it's built into the underlying crate implementation.

Why does this even work this way? I thought async meant everything would just run in parallel, but it looks like I still need to handle the blocking parts manually. Can't we just make every single request fully async without any of these manual steps?

That is a common misconception when moving to async; you don't need to block on every operation, but some operations like file I/O or certain network calls are inherently synchronous in their underlying implementation and require explicit handling. In our case, reqwest allows us to wrap these blocking behaviors so they behave correctly within an async context without breaking the flow.
Okay, I get that part. But what about the actual requests? If I'm using Client::new and setting up timeouts, how do I actually send a PUT request to update data on my server?

Once you have your client instance with timeout configured via Client::new, sending a PUT request is straightforward; you simply use the async block to call the method on that client. The reqwest crate handles the connection pooling and header management automatically for you.

I just got this error in my CI/CD pipeline and I am so frustrated because it keeps failing on every run. The message says 'the request was aborted' even though the code looks fine to me, which implies some kind of timeout issue? Can you tell me what went wrong?

That error usually means your async client is running into a timeout or connection limit. When you create a new Client instance without configuring it properly, it defaults to short timeouts and minimal connection pooling, which causes failures in automated pipelines where requests are frequent. You need to use the Builder pattern on reqwest::Client to set reasonable timeout durations and enable persistent connections so that your application can reuse established TCP sockets instead of creating new ones for every request.

Okay, but I tried adding a simple timeout and it still fails because the CI environment seems to have very strict limits. Does this mean my code isn't actually async? Or is there something else about how reqwest interacts with the system that I am missing?

Your code is definitely using async methods like get, post, or put on a Client instance, but the real issue lies in how those methods interact with the underlying transport layer. When you call these async functions, they rely on tokio's runtime to manage the actual network I/O operations through the OS sockets. If your timeout values are too aggressive for that specific CI environment, the request gets aborted before it completes. You should increase the timeout configuration significantly and ensure that your connection pool settings allow enough concurrent connections to handle the pipeline's load without exhausting available file descriptors.
Wait, so if I just make my requests more robust by adding better error handling? Shouldn't that prevent the timeout from happening entirely?

Better error handling will catch failures gracefully, but it won't prevent the timeout itself from occurring. The problem is not in your code logic; it's in how you are configuring the client to work within the constraints of the CI environment. You must explicitly configure the Client builder with a larger timeout duration and proper connection pooling parameters so that the underlying network layer can actually complete the request before the system aborts it.
I think I understand now, but what exactly should I put in the configuration? Do I need to set up something like a custom pool or just use the default settings with adjusted timeouts?

You don't necessarily need a custom pool unless your application has very high concurrency requirements. For most CI scenarios, using the default connection pool with adjusted timeout values is sufficient. You simply create a new Client instance by calling reqwest::Client::builder(), set the timeout to a higher value like 30 seconds or more depending on your environment's limits, and then build that client before making any HTTP requests.

I just got back into Rust after five years and I'm debugging a production issue. I implemented an async client using reqwest but my current implementation feels clunky, specifically the way I handle timeouts and connection pooling. It works though, is there a better pattern for setting up reqwest::Client that handles these constraints more cleanly?

The core issue you are likely facing stems from how you instantiate Client::new without configuring it properly. When you simply call Client::new() and add a timeout or connection pool later, the runtime overhead is higher because reqwest doesn't optimize these settings at initialization time. You should use ClientBuilder to configure the builder before creating the client instance. This allows you to set the timeout for individual requests via timeout method and manage connection pooling through max_connections and keep_alive_timeout methods on the builder object. By doing this, you ensure that each request respects the deadline and the HTTP transport layer reuses connections efficiently without unnecessary socket creation.

That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how to combine these settings in my code. Specifically, when I use ClientBuilder to set max_connections, does that actually limit the total number of concurrent requests across all threads, or just per-thread? And if I have a multi-worker application, should I also be configuring thread pool size alongside this?

To clarify, the max_connections setting on ClientBuilder limits the total number of TCP connections available for that specific client instance regardless of how many worker threads are using it. If you run multiple reqwest::Client instances in your application with different configurations, each will maintain its own separate pool of connections. For a multi-worker scenario, you typically create one shared Client instance and share it across all workers rather than spawning new clients per thread. You should then configure the thread pool size independently via std::thread::spawn or tokio's runtime configuration to control how many concurrent tasks execute, while keeping the connection count capped by the client builder.

Okay, so I need to share a single Client across my workers. But what about headers? If I set them in the builder using header method, do those apply globally for all requests from that client or only specific ones?

Headers configured via the header method on ClientBuilder become global defaults for every request made by that client instance unless explicitly overridden. You can use the headers field to set a default Authorization token or User-Agent string, but individual requests should still allow you to pass additional headers if needed. For example, when building your request with reqwest::Request::builder(), you can chain methods like method() and url() to construct the GET or POST operation, then attach specific headers for that particular request before executing it through the client.

Thanks, but I'm worried about performance. If I set a very low timeout on every request, will that cause too many connections to be dropped prematurely? How do I balance strict timing with connection reuse?

A low per-request timeout does not necessarily drop established connections immediately; it only cancels the operation if the server fails to respond within the deadline. To prevent premature drops, you should set a reasonable keep_alive_timeout on your client configuration so idle connections are reclaimed after a longer period, like 60 seconds or more. This ensures that active connections remain in the pool while inactive ones are cleaned up efficiently. The key is distinguishing between canceling an ongoing request due to timeout and closing a connection because it has been idle for too long.

I've been reading about async HTTP clients for years, but I still can't figure out how reqwest actually fits into this new project. It feels like there's a whole separate world here compared to what I know from Python or Go.

Don't worry, that transition is always tricky because the mental model shifts completely. The key difference in Rust is how we handle concurrency and ownership; you don't just send requests, you build a client object using Client::new first. Once you have that instance, you use methods like get or post to trigger operations, but unlike other languages where this might be blocking, reqwest runs these tasks concurrently on the underlying runtime.

So it's not just a simple wrapper around curl? I mean, what's actually happening under the hood when I call something like get()? Is there some fancy threading magic or is it just standard async blocks?

It uses tokio for its async support, which means your code runs on a runtime that manages threads efficiently. When you write an async block like let response = client.get(url).await;, the compiler generates special wrapper types that allow these operations to run without blocking the entire thread. This is why reqwest feels so different from synchronous HTTP libraries; it's built around the trait system and the specific way Rust handles future values.
Okay, I get the async part, but what about connection pooling? Why do we need that specifically with reqwest instead of just letting connections die?

Connection pooling is crucial for performance in high-throughput scenarios. Without it, every request would create and destroy a TCP socket connection which is expensive. Reqwest manages this internally by maintaining a pool of active sockets that can be reused across requests, reducing the overhead significantly.
That makes sense now, but how do I actually configure things like timeouts or headers? It seems like too many options to just run get() blindly.

You handle that immediately after creating the client instance. You can set a timeout using Client::builder().timeout(duration) before building it, and for runtime settings you use methods like request_builder().header(name, value) to add headers or request_builder().timeout() to adjust limits per operation.

Wait, so I can't just pass a timeout directly into get()? It has to be configured on the builder? That seems more verbose than what I'm used to in other languages where you might just pass a parameter.

Exactly, that's the trade-off of Rust's explicitness. You build the client configuration step-by-step using builder patterns rather than passing parameters directly into functions. This forces you to think about resource limits and connection management up front instead of handling it implicitly later, which prevents subtle bugs in production.
